schools.summary: Gill: these words which follow are commonly called the decalogue, or ten commands, a system or body of laws selected and adapted to the case of the people of Israel; the witnesses note that God spake all these words, the great law about to be given — the solemn opening of the giving of the Ten Commandments, spoken by God himself (the law coming from the mouth of God: 'And God spake all these words, saying'; the author, 'God spake,' that these laws were uttered by God himself, with his own voice, from the midst of the fire and the cloud at Sinai, not delivered by Moses only nor by an angel, the dignity of the giver marking the weight of the law; the fulness, 'all these words,' the whole Decalogue, the ten commands, a complete body of laws, the sum of the moral law; the abiding character of this law the commentators draw, that it is the eternal rule of righteousness, binding on all men, summed up by Christ in love to God and neighbour; the awful solemnity of God's own voice proclaiming his law to the assembled people; that the law's coming from God himself binds the conscience to obey).

themes: And God spake all these words
saying
God himself speaking all the words of the Decalogue with his own voice
the eternal moral law, summed by Christ in love to God and neighbour, binding all to obey

schools.summary: Gill: this verse does not contain the first of the commands but is a preface to them, showing that God had a right to enact and enjoin the people of Israel laws; the witnesses note God prefacing the law with his name, the LORD their God, who had brought them out of Egypt and the house of bondage — the foundation laid for the law, God's authority and his redeeming love (God first declaring who he is before he commands: 'I am the LORD thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage'; that this is a preface to the commands, not itself the first, showing that God had a right to enact and enjoin laws on Israel; the ground of his authority, 'I am the LORD thy God,' that as Jehovah, the self-existent and almighty God, and their covenant God, he had the right to command and they the duty to obey; the ground of his claim on their love and obedience, 'which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage,' that he had redeemed them from cruel slavery, and so by the right of redemption they were his, bound to serve him; the gospel order the commentators mark, that redemption goes before the law, mercy before duty, the redeemed obeying out of gratitude; that God's people obey not to be saved but because they are saved).

debates.question: What is the significance of the preface to the Ten Commandments?

sides.position: It grounds the law in God's authority (Jehovah, their covenant God) and his redeeming love (having brought them out of bondage) — redemption going before the law, mercy before duty; the redeemed obey out of gratitude, not to be saved but because they are saved

themes: I am the LORD thy God
which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage
the preface grounding the law in God's authority and redeeming love
redemption going before the law, the redeemed obeying out of gratitude

schools.summary: Gill: this is the first command, opposed to the polytheism of the Gentiles, the Egyptians from whom Israel was just come, requiring that God alone be acknowledged and worshipped; the witnesses note the first commandment forbidding any other gods before the LORD — the foundation of all true religion, that God alone is to be owned and worshipped (the first command claiming God's sole right to be God: 'Thou shalt have no other gods before me'; the matter of the command, that it forbids all other gods, all the false deities of the heathen, especially the gods of Egypt just left and of Canaan, and requires that the LORD alone be acknowledged as God; the force of 'before me,' or before my face, that to set up any other god is done in God's very sight and presence, an affront to him who sees all; the breadth of the command the commentators draw, that it forbids not only gross idolatry but all that puts anything in God's place in the heart, whether self, the world, riches, or pleasure, every idol of the soul; that this first command is the foundation of all the rest, for he who truly has the LORD for his God will keep all his commandments; that God will share his glory and worship with no other).

themes: Thou shalt have no other gods
before me
the first commandment, that God alone is to be owned and worshipped
forbidding not only gross idolatry but every idol of the heart, done before God's very face

schools.summary: Gill: thou shalt not make any image of anything graven by art or man's device, cut out of wood or stone, or anything molten, or cast into a mould; the witnesses note the second commandment forbidding any graven image or likeness of things in heaven, earth, or sea — the prohibition of all images in the worship of God (the second command guarding the manner of God's worship: 'Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth'; the breadth of the prohibition, 'any graven image, or any likeness of any thing,' that no image at all, whether graven, carved of wood or stone, or molten, cast of metal, of any creature in heaven, in the earth, in the water, might be made to represent or worship God; the reason the commentators give, that God, being a Spirit, infinite and invisible, cannot be likened to any creature, and to make an image of him is to dishonour and debase him; that the command forbids both the making of idols of false gods and, especially, the worshipping of the true God by images, the corrupting of his worship; that God will be worshipped as he has appointed, not after the inventions of men).

themes: Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing that is in heaven above
or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth
the second commandment forbidding images in the worship of God
God, being a Spirit, cannot be likened to any creature, his worship not to be corrupted by images

schools.summary: Gill: thou shalt not perform any worship to them, show any reverence by any gesture of the body, bowing being put for all other acts of worship; the witnesses note God forbidding to bow down to or serve images, for he is a jealous God, visiting the fathers' iniquity on the children to the third and fourth generation of them that hate him — the prohibition of idolatrous worship and the awful reason of God's jealousy (God guarding his worship with a solemn threat: 'Thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God'; the act forbidden, 'bow down thyself to them, nor serve them,' that no worship at all, neither outward gesture nor inward service, might be given to images; the reason, 'for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God,' that God, like a husband jealous for his wife's love, will not endure a rival in the worship and devotion of his people but is jealous for his own honour; the threat, 'visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children... of them that hate me,' that God would punish idolatry, that great sin and hatred of him, even visiting its effects on the children of the guilty who followed their fathers' sin, to the third and fourth generation; that idolatry is hatred of God, the breaking of the marriage-bond between him and his people).

themes: Thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God
visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children... of them that hate me
God forbidding all worship of images, jealous for his honour
idolatry being hatred of God, the breaking of the marriage-bond between him and his people

schools.summary: Gill: God shows mercy to thousands of them that love him, and show their love by worshipping God, and him only, serving him acceptably with reverence and godly fear, by a cheerful obedience; the witnesses note God promising to show mercy to thousands of them that love him and keep his commandments — the gracious counterpart to the threat, the abundant mercy promised to the obedient (against the threat to idolaters God setting a far greater promise to his lovers: 'And shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me, and keep my commandments'; the contrast the commentators mark, that the threat reached but to the third and fourth generation of haters, but the mercy to thousands of generations of lovers, God's mercy far outrunning his judgment; the persons, 'them that love me, and keep my commandments,' that true love to God is proved by keeping his commandments, as Christ said, 'If ye love me, keep my commandments,' love and obedience joined; the abundance, 'shewing mercy unto thousands,' that God delights to show mercy, and that to a vast multitude and through long generations, to all who love and obey him; the comfort, that the obedient and their seed are heirs of God's covenant mercy).

themes: And shewing mercy unto thousands
of them that love me, and keep my commandments
God's mercy to thousands of generations, far outrunning the threat to the few
true love proved by keeping his commandments, love and obedience joined
